the_one_thing.statement: Graphs are organized ways to show data, and that every part of a graph (title, categories, key, scale) tells us something specific we need to read accurately.

critical_misconception.label: Graph as Picture
critical_misconception.why_critical: Students treat graphs as literal drawings rather than data representations, missing that each element carries specific meaning.

the_one_thing.success_indicator: Student correctly answers 'how many more/fewer' questions by reading graph values and subtracting (not by visual estimation or treating the graph as a picture).
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: Students who don't solidify 1:1 scale understanding will struggle when M2 introduces scaled graphs where each symbol represents multiple items.

student_facing_goal: You'll learn to read graphs—finding patterns, comparing categories, and answering questions about what the data shows.
exit_check_tests: Can student read values from picture graphs and bar graphs (1:1 scale)?
Can student identify 'most,' 'least,' and compare categories?
Can student solve 'how many more/fewer' problems using graph data?
Can student solve 'in all' problems using graph data?
learning_goals: Interpret picture graphs and bar graphs to generate questions about the data.
Represent data using bar graphs and picture graphs. Solve one- and two-step problems using addition and subtraction with information from bar graphs.

vocabulary_staging.approach: Use naturally during data collection and graph creation; informal reinforcement of Grade 2 terms
Explicitly name graph components as students examine pre-made graphs; 'The KEY tells us what each symbol means'
Introduce when transitioning from picture graphs to bar graphs; compare/contrast the two formats
Explicitly teach during problem-solving; 'How many more means find the DIFFERENCE—we subtract'
Use in question contexts; students demonstrate understanding through problem-solving
Expect natural student use; consolidate understanding

bridge.from_grade_2: Students created and interpreted single-unit picture graphs and bar graphs. They solved simple comparison problems. However, Grade 2 work was often collaborative (class graphs, physical manipulatives) and may not have emphasized independent interpretation or the specific language of 'how many more/fewer.'
bridge.this_module: Students activate and consolidate Grade 2 knowledge through: creating ONE personal graph (Warmup) to establish ownership; interpreting multiple pre-made graphs (Lesson) to build reading fluency; explicitly learning that 'how many more/fewer' means subtract; and practicing with both horizontal and vertical orientations.
bridge.to_module_2: Students will apply their solid 1:1 understanding when M2 introduces the critical concept: each symbol can represent MORE than one item (scaled graphs with 1:2 scale). The 'how many more/fewer' language established here carries forward.

advanced_concepts: Scaled graphs where each symbol represents more than 1 item (Module 2)
Data collection methods beyond the provided game
Independent graph creation in Lesson (Module 2+ skill)
required_phrases: A graph isn't just a picture to look at—every part gives us information to READ
We find the DIFFERENCE by subtracting
'How many fewer' is ALSO a comparison
'In all' means we want the TOTAL. We ADD to find totals.
You found exactly what you need

forbidden_phrases.reason: All M1 graphs have exact 1:1 values

misconceptions.description: Students treat graphs as literal drawings rather than data representations, missing that each element carries specific meaning. Trigger behavior: student focuses on visual appearance of graph rather than data values; may describe graph shapes ('it goes up') rather than data meaning ('more people chose cats'). Prevention: explicitly name graph components and their purpose; ask 'What does this graph TELL us?' not 'What do you SEE?'; model reading the key; use varied symbols to emphasize that the COUNT matters, not the symbol type.
prevention: Explicitly name graph components and their PURPOSE
Ask 'What does this graph TELL us?' not 'What do you SEE?'
Use varied symbols to emphasize that the COUNT matters, not the symbol type

misconceptions.description: When asked 'How many more chose cats than dogs?', student incorporates ALL categories into their answer. Trigger behavior: student uses data from irrelevant categories when a question only requires two. Prevention: model question analysis to identify which categories a question requires BEFORE solving; include questions that deliberately don't use all categories; visually dim irrelevant categories during remediation.
prevention: Model question analysis: identify which categories a question requires BEFORE solving
Include questions that deliberately DON'T use all categories
Visually dim irrelevant categories during remediation

phases.purpose: Activate prior knowledge of data collection and graphing from Grade 2. Build ownership by having students create a graph with their collected data before the Lesson focuses on interpretation.

variables.format: Data Collection Game → Symbol Selection → Graph Creation

phases.purpose: Activate prior knowledge of picture graphs while explicitly naming graph components and their purposes. Build confidence through multiple reading opportunities before introducing bar graphs.
Introduce bar graphs as another way to show the same information. Explicitly connect bar height/length to values. Practice with both orientations.

Explicitly teach that 'how many more/fewer' means compare by subtracting. Teach 'in all' means add for total. Introduce two-step problems as sequential submissions. Prevent Misconception #17 (All Data Must Be Used).

Verify Lesson understanding before Practice. Tests whether student can read graph values, identify most/least, solve 'how many more/fewer' problems, and solve 'in all' problems using graphs taught in Lesson.

Consolidate understanding that picture graphs and bar graphs are two ways to show the same data. Connect graph reading to real-world contexts. Reflect on problem-solving strategies. This is NOT more practice — it is connection-making and consolidation.
